title: The Catalogue of Nearlys
caption: On the protective intelligence hidden inside the lives we almost lived.
artworkAlt: A solitary figure standing in a stark, open landscape
body: There’s a gallery I keep where no one else is allowed to go. Walk its corridors and you’ll find them hung in long rows, the almost lives, each one framed and titled and never quite finished. The degree I didn’t take, the love I left before it could leave me. The work that was thriving the very week I walked away from it. The friendships I let go cold for reasons I couldn’t have named at the time. Most of us keep a gallery like this, those of us who began the way I did. We are a people of the nearly, the not quite, the if only. We bounce from one bright thing to the next. We call it restlessness, or bad luck, or a flaw in the character we were born with. The world tends to nod along with that last one. But I’ve come to believe it is none of them; I think it is a wound, doing exactly what it was made to do.
fullBody:
There’s a gallery I keep where no one else is allowed to go. Walk its corridors and you’ll find them hung in long rows, the almost lives, each one framed and titled and never quite finished. The degree I didn’t take, the love I left before it could leave me. The work that was thriving the very week I walked away from it. The friendships I let go cold for reasons I couldn’t have named at the time. Most of us keep a gallery like this, those of us who began the way I did. We are a people of the nearly, the not quite, the if only. We bounce from one bright thing to the next. We call it restlessness, or bad luck, or a flaw in the character we were born with. The world tends to nod along with that last one. But I’ve come to believe it is none of them; I think it is a wound, doing exactly what it was made to do.

To understand it, you have to start with the story they told about us. We were raised inside that story the way a fish is raised inside water, never once seeing the thing it breathes. The story goes like this: we started badly and we ended well. We were the rescued, the chosen, the fortunate ones lifted out of one life and set down softly in a kinder one. And the only fitting response to a rescue is gratitude, so gratitude became the air in the house, unspoken and total. It sealed our mouths before we’d even learned there might be something we needed to say. You cannot grieve a thing you’ve been instructed to be thankful for, and you cannot hold a funeral at your own rescue. So the grief went elsewhere, the way grief always does, and it found other doors.

Here is one of the doors it finds: we become the people who can build the whole cathedral. Then, on the morning it’s finished, we stand in the nave with a lit match in our hand. We reach the very lip of the good thing, the job, the marriage, the belonging we swore was all we ever wanted. And something old and wordless in us bends down and picks up a stone. From the outside this looks like self sabotage, a kind of stupidity, a stubborn refusal to be happy. What the outside can’t see is a child who learned the hardest lesson a human being can learn. They learned it before there were any words to cushion the blow. That the very people you need most can hand you to a stranger and walk away. After a lesson like that, leaving first isn’t madness. It’s strategy. We leave before we can be left, and we burn the house down ourselves so that no one can ever take it from us. It is the most logical thing in the world, and it has cost some of us nearly everything we had.

And there’s the other face of the same wound, which is the reaching. If some of us run, others of us chase. We throw ourselves at edges, at speed, at strangers, at anything carrying enough voltage to be felt through the numbness. A body that has been braced since birth will choose danger over emptiness every time, because at least danger is proof that you’re still here. We move through lovers like rooms in a house we are searching. We are looking for the one face that was taken from us before we could fix it in memory. We never quite find it, and we never quite stop. We bounce, as they like to say, from one thing to the next. They mistake the bouncing for appetite, or recklessness, or a failure to commit. It is none of those either; it is a search party. It is a small person still hunting, in every new face and every fresh thrill, for the first connection that was severed. They are trying to prove, over and over and over, that this time they will be the one who gets to stay.

This isn’t only poetry: the shadow of it shows up in the cold arithmetic, and the arithmetic only ever leans one way. Adopted people are close to four times more likely to attempt suicide than those who were kept. That gap stubbornly remains even after researchers strip out every other risk they can think to measure. Walk into a clinic for substance treatment, and you will find us there. We turn up in numbers wildly disproportionate to how few of us there are in the world at all. I want to be careful here, because by most measures most of us are fine. We hold down jobs and raise our children and laugh at the dinner table. The effect sizes the studies turn up are, on paper, small. But small and relentless is its own kind of devastation. It means there is a current running beneath all of us, and for some it pulls them the whole way under. We are not weak. We have simply been swimming, our entire lives, against something the kept have never once had to feel against their legs.

And when the mind refuses to carry a thing, the body quietly volunteers to hold it instead. Ask a room full of adoptees about their stomachs and watch the hands rise. We are a people of knotted guts and unaccountable nausea, of the churn that no scan and no test ever fully explains. For years I thought this was coincidence. It is not. The newest research takes infant animals and separates them from their mothers. What it discovers is that the gut itself, the long wiring that runs between belly and brain, grows differently after that severing. It is tuned for the rest of its life toward alarm and toward pain. In human children, any early rupture at all lifts the odds of exactly these troubles, so sit with that for a moment. The single experiment that best explains our aching stomachs is an experiment about taking the newborn away from the mother. The body kept the ledger that our memory was far too young to write, and it has been quietly settling the account every day since.

There is one more room in the gallery, and this one is lined with mirrors that never quite hold a reflection. It’s where we keep the imposter, the one who walks into every room already certain of being found out and asked to leave. Of course we carry this; we were edited out of our own first chapter. The opening pages of nearly everyone else’s story are simply there, assumed. A mother’s face, a family name, a long line of people reaching back into the dark. Ours were lifted out and replaced. And however tender the replacement was, some animal part of us has always known the book was rebound by another hand. So we perform belonging the way an actor performs a part, word perfect and never once at home in it. We stay forever braced for the tap on the shoulder that tells us the role was never really ours.

For most of my life the question hanging over me was the wrong question. What is wrong with you. It hummed under every report card and every collapsed relationship, under every doctor’s helpless shrug. What is wrong with you. And the true answer to that question is nothing. There is nothing wrong with us. The right question, the one almost no one ever thought to kneel down and ask, is softer, and it changes everything it touches. Not what is wrong with you. What happened to you. Because the moment you ask the second question, the entire catalogue rearranges itself before your eyes. The leaving, the numbing, the chasing, the burning, the bracing, the churning gut, the long trail of nearlys. None of it is damage. All of it is design. These are the strategies of an infant nervous system that was handed an impossible equation before it could speak a single word. It solved that equation the only way left open, by staying ready, staying armoured, staying always within one stride of the door. We were not broken by what happened to us; we were shaped by it. We adapted, and we adapted brilliantly. The world proved to us early and without appeal that safety could be taken away in a single afternoon.

So I don’t call it sabotage anymore when I set the good thing down and walk; I call it an old friend. The one who kept me alive when I was far too small to keep myself, still faithfully reporting for a war that ended decades ago. Still standing guard at a door that no one is coming through. I’m learning, slowly and imperfectly, to thank that friend, and to tell it that it can finally rest. That is the closest thing to healing I have found. Not the repair of something that was never once faulty, but the long, late work of understanding it. And if you have read this far, and you are not one of us, then I will leave you with one thing. It is the only thing I would ever ask you to carry out of here. Picture one of us standing at the very edge of our own good fortune, reaching down for the match. When you next meet that person, do not ask what is wrong. Ask us what happened. And then do the bravest thing of all, the thing the world has never once managed to do for us. Stay long enough to hear the whole of the answer.
beforeReading: This essay reads patterns such as leaving, chasing, bracing, gut distress, and imposture through the lens of early separation. It combines personal reflection with references to research, but it isn’t a diagnosis or a claim that every adoptee shares the same outcomes. It asks whether behaviours commonly judged as sabotage may have begun as protection.
originTitle: The unfinished lives formed their own private gallery.
origin: The piece began with the lives I nearly lived: the work, love, study, friendship, and belonging I approached, then sometimes left before they could leave me. The gallery gave those unfinished lives a place to stand together. Once I stopped asking what was wrong with me, the pattern became less like evidence for the prosecution and more like an old survival strategy continuing beyond the conditions that created it.
meaningTitle: Understanding changes the question before it changes the pattern.
meaning: Writing it allowed me to distinguish explanation from excuse and adaptation from defect. The leaving, chasing, bracing, and performing don’t become harmless simply because they once protected me, but they become intelligible. That change matters. Shame asks me to destroy the guard. Understanding lets me thank him, notice when he is no longer needed, and begin choosing whether the match stays unlit.
recognitionTitle: Readers recognised their own rooms in the gallery.
recognitionIntro: Selected responses from adoptee readers when this essay was first shared in the Adoptology community.
seoTitle: The Catalogue of Nearlys | Jonathan Lyon
seoDescription: An adoptee testimony essay on leaving first, protective intelligence, attachment, belonging, and the unfinished lives gathered inside the word nearly.
